<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] The zone mixes production source files with their unit tests, which is appropriate given the small footprint and keeps test proximity high.
[observation] [info] shared-types.ts suggests some type definitions are scoped to this zone rather than the broader server types; ensure these types don't need to be promoted to the web package's public schema as usage consumers grow.
[observation] [info] task-usage.ts is the sole entry point for the zone; all consumers should import through this surface rather than reaching into incremental-task-usage.ts directly to maintain encapsulation.
[pattern] [warning] The cleanup scheduler (usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts) is a stateful process-level concern but is absent from monorepo-integration-tests — if the scheduler interacts with rex or sourcevision stores at startup, that contract is currently untested at the integration boundary
[anti-pattern] [warning] shared-types.ts defines types consumed cross-zone by web-dashboard but lives inside the analytics zone with no explicit export contract. Consumers reach into zone-internal types rather than through a stable interface boundary, making the contract implicit and fragile if the zone is ever refactored.
[suggestion] [warning] The zone exports types through shared-types.ts directly rather than through a gateway module. As the web package grows, add a thin re-export gateway (analytics-gateway.ts) in web-dashboard that proxies analytics types, mirroring the rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts pattern already established in the codebase.
[suggestion] [info] shared-types.ts defines the cross-zone type contract but carries no stability annotation (e.g. @public, @internal, or a semver comment). Add an explicit contract marker so callers know when breaking changes require coordinated updates across both zones.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
- Cohesion of 0.87 with a focused 8-file footprint indicates the zone has a clear, singular responsibility — usage metering and retention
- The cleanup scheduler registration (register-scheduler.ts, usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts) and the incremental snapshot service (incremental-task-usage.ts) are distinct sub-concerns; extracting them would only be warranted if the zone grows significantly
- Shared-types.ts acting as a local type contract keeps the zone's interface stable; verify it does not duplicate types already defined in the broader web server type surface
- The zone mixes production source files with their unit tests, which is appropriate given the small footprint and keeps test proximity high.
- task-usage.ts is the sole entry point for the zone; all consumers should import through this surface rather than reaching into incremental-task-usage.ts directly to maintain encapsulation.
- shared-types.ts suggests some type definitions are scoped to this zone rather than the broader server types; ensure these types don't need to be promoted to the web package's public schema as usage consumers grow.
- task-usage-analytics has zero coupling to the monorepo test zones — the analytics retention and scheduler logic is validated only by its internal unit tests, not by any cross-package integration test; a gap exists in the outer test ring for scheduler lifecycle verification across package boundaries
- The cleanup scheduler (usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts) is a stateful process-level concern but is absent from monorepo-integration-tests — if the scheduler interacts with rex or sourcevision stores at startup, that contract is currently untested at the integration boundary
- register-scheduler.ts couples the analytics zone to the web server startup sequence. If the scheduler registration call is conditional or order-dependent, failures will manifest at runtime rather than at import resolution time — a class of bug not caught by either the unit tests or the integration test.
- 3 of 8 files (37.5%) are test files, which is a high ratio suggesting the production surface of this zone is thin relative to its test harness. If the zone grows, the test files should graduate to a dedicated sub-directory.
- shared-types.ts defines types consumed cross-zone by web-dashboard but lives inside the analytics zone with no explicit export contract. Consumers reach into zone-internal types rather than through a stable interface boundary, making the contract implicit and fragile if the zone is ever refactored.
- The zone's external type contract (shared-types.ts) flows upward into web-dashboard but the type file has no version guard or stability annotation. If shared-types.ts changes, web-dashboard consumers break silently — there is no compile-time contract that alerts callers to the change until a build fails.
- The 3:1 production-to-test file ratio (5 source, 3 test) is inverted from the typical pattern where source files outnumber tests. This suggests the zone may be over-tested at the unit level while lacking higher-level integration coverage for its scheduler lifecycle.
- shared-types.ts defines the cross-zone type contract but carries no stability annotation (e.g. @public, @internal, or a semver comment). Add an explicit contract marker so callers know when breaking changes require coordinated updates across both zones.
- The zone exports types through shared-types.ts directly rather than through a gateway module. As the web package grows, add a thin re-export gateway (analytics-gateway.ts) in web-dashboard that proxies analytics types, mirroring the rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts pattern already established in the codebase.
- [call graph] 114 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)

</insights>
